It should say FluidAudio, not fluid audio, so the dictionary bias has to catch it.

The Qwen model is the speed and accuracy sweet spot, but the larger one wins on recall.
I have found Gemini Flash to be good enough for the rewrite pass once the secrets are tokenized out.

The Nginx config proxies the request to the Redis cache layer before it hits the app.
Check whether the flag is at one hundred percent before you remove the legacy branch.
I think we need to remove the translations, they are not used anywhere anymore.
The selective tests went flaky three times, so I escalated it instead of burning the retry budget.
Pin the dependency to the commit, the latest release has the empty output bug.
Run EXPLAIN on the query and see if it is doing a sequential scan on that column.
The index is missing on the foreign key, that is why the join is slow.
The token to original map stays in memory only, it is never logged or written to history.
Verbatim spans get tokenized first, and redaction tokens get added last, just before the rewrite.
Moonshine has no on-device bias path, so I badged it as no dictionary bias.
The MLX metallib is a hard runtime requirement, Qwen crashes without it.
Paste is the primary insertion path, it lands across Electron and native apps and undoes in one keystroke.
The Globe key and right Option are both capturable and distinguishable through the event tap.
Parakeet bias is constrained CTC keyword spotting, not a blind post-processing rescorer.
Whisper bias breaks in every stock release because of the prompt token bug.
When the privacy toggle is on, the context checkboxes are forced off and locked.
The post rewrite gate checks that every redaction token came back exactly once, or it falls back locally.
Schedule a sync with Rajesh and Anya next Tuesday and send the agenda ahead of time.
Ask Sofia whether the design review can slip to Thursday morning.
Loop in Dmitri and Yuki on the thread, they own that part of the system.
I will introduce you to Hassan, he has the most context on the older code.
The build finished in 4 minutes and 32 seconds, down from 11 minutes yesterday.
Resident memory sits around 38 megabytes, and the transcribe call is roughly 80 milliseconds.
Open parenthesis, see the footnote, close parenthesis, then continue the sentence.
Reschedule to 2026-06-30 at 4 p.m., and cancel the 10:15 if it conflicts.
The patient presented with tachycardia and mild dyspnea overnight.
Send the subpoena to opposing counsel by end of day.
The espresso machine needs a new portafilter gasket and a backflush.
We tuned the carburetor and replaced the alternator on the old wagon.

When I dictate a long passage I want the engine to keep up without dropping words, handle the natural pauses where I stop to think, and still get the domain terms right, things like Kubernetes and Postgres and FluidAudio, even when I am talking quickly and running sentences together the way I actually do when I am heads down on a problem.

Here is where I landed after the weekend. I found a much smaller model that is memory efficient, I got word bias working across every engine, and I wired the whole pipeline together so a dictation goes from speech, to the text stages, to an optional rewrite, and inserts atomically. It still takes a moment when there is a rewrite in the loop, but it is good enough that I have been using it every single day.

The plan is straightforward even if it is tedious. I am going to land the early pull requests first, get reviews while the later steps are still being figured out, and give a rough sense of timelines without pretending the hard part is solved. I do not know exactly how long the last step will take, so I would rather say that out loud than guess and be wrong later.

I am going to walk through my reasoning so you can poke holes in it. We could rebake the primary key, but that touches every row and it is a large table, so the cost is real. The alternative avoids the extra hop entirely, which is why I am leaning that way, but I am not attached to it, and if you see a problem I am missing I would genuinely rather hear it now than after we ship.

Recording for a benchmark is more work than people expect. You read each line exactly as written, save it as its own clip, keep your normal microphone and your normal pace, and then you re-record a handful of them in a slightly noisy room so you have something to measure robustness against. It is an afternoon of talking plus the time to verify every transcript, which is why I am building it up in tiers instead of all at once.

The privacy story is the part I care about most. Speech recognition is always on device, there is no cloud transcription ever, and the only thing that can leave the machine is an optional rewrite that I key myself. Before that call goes out, anything sensitive is tokenized into a nonce, the rewrite happens over the redacted text, and the original is restored locally afterward. There is no telemetry and no analytics, none of it is collected.

Let me give you the full picture in one go, because I keep explaining it in pieces and it comes out scattered. The product is a local first dictation tool for the Mac. You hold a key, you talk, you let go, and the text is inserted into whatever app you are in, all in a single undoable action. Recognition runs entirely on device, so your voice never leaves the machine. There are several engines you can choose from, Parakeet, Whisper, Qwen, Moonshine and a couple of others, and each one supports a dictionary so that when I say FluidAudio or WhisperKit or Kubernetes it comes out spelled correctly instead of as three lowercase words. On top of recognition there is an optional rewrite step that uses a language model, but that is the only thing that ever touches the network, and only after the sensitive parts of the transcript have been swapped out for placeholder tokens. The rewrite happens over that redacted text, the model never sees the real values, and the placeholders are filled back in locally once it returns. If a single placeholder does not come back exactly once, the whole thing falls back to the local transcript and tells me, because a dropped token would either leak something I redacted or corrupt the part I wanted left alone. That gate is a hard check, not a nice to have. The rest is configuration. A mode is just a named bag of settings that a generic pipeline runs, so adding a new one is data, never code, and I can route to different prompts based on a trigger phrase at the end of what I say. It is the kind of thing I would never have finished without leaning hard on the robots, and honestly it is the most fun I have had building something in a long time.
